frame.description: The product outcome and boundary of the workstream.

layers.label: Layers
layers.description: The enforcement model and owner boundaries.

lifecycle.label: Lifecycle
lifecycle.description: How agents and CI use the harness end to end.

caveats.label: Caveats
caveats.description: Known tradeoffs, unfinished layers, and future work.

slides.explanation:
## What Habitat Is

Habitat is the repo's structural operating system: it turns architecture from scattered scripts, tests, configs, and prose into one executable, ratchetable contract.

explainer.content: The core shift is not "we added another lint wrapper." The shift is that repository structure now has a named lifecycle. Agents can ask what owns a path, generate supported shapes, run the right checks, and get normalized diagnostics tied to explicit baselines. The harness encodes the architecture the repo already implied; it does not invent product architecture or runtime behavior.

items.label: Slices closed
items.subtitle: Nx, scaffold, boundaries, Biome, oclif, Grit, consolidation, hooks, generators

items.label: Harness rules
items.subtitle: Final H8 habitat:check count; zero failing, one advisory

slides.explanation:
## Why This Exists

Before Habitat, enforcement was real but fragmented. Rules lived in lint scripts, ESLint blocks, architecture tests, CI jobs, docs, and AGENTS guidance. The system worked only when an agent already knew where to look.

table.caption: The migration target was scattered enforcement, not a greenfield tool.
rows: Before Habitat,After Habitat
Nine lint scripts with different outputs,Rule-pack entries with normalized diagnostics
ESLint mixed style and architecture concerns,ESLint quarantined to Nx module boundaries only
Structural checks hidden in ordinary tests,Structural checks ported or slimmed after parity proof
Generated-zone promises in prose,Generated-zone file-layer gates
Manual project/rule scaffolding,Native Nx generators for supported shapes
Agents infer verification from habit,Agents classify first and run reported targets

blocks.explanation: The product outcome is an agent-operable repo: classify first, generate what has a known shape, author business logic, then verify through the same structural contract CI will understand.

slides.explanation:
## The Layer Model

Each structural concern has one owning layer. That boundary is the main design achievement.

layers.title: Five Enforcement Layers

layers.label: Repository graph
layers.description: Workspace graph, tags, affected targets, cache, generators, and migrations.

layers.label: Project imports
layers.description: Graph-level dependency law through a minimal ESLint boundary config.

layers.label: Hygiene
layers.description: Formatting, import organization, ordinary lint hygiene, and safe assists.

layers.label: Source shape
layers.description: Native Grit patterns for syntax and path-aware guards for generated zones.

layers.label: Contract surface
layers.description: Rule-pack semantics, baselines, JSON diagnostics, classify, hooks, fix, and verify.

layers.caption: The harness composes native tools; it does not collapse them into bespoke scripts.

slides.explanation:
## The Ratchet

A rule is not just a check. It has an owner, a baseline, a failure interpretation, and a burn-down path.

diagram.content:
flowchart TD
  A["Rule enters rule pack"] --> B["Baseline recorded"]
  B --> C{"Baseline empty?"}
  C -->|Yes| D["Locked rule: any finding fails"]
  C -->|No| E["Existing findings frozen"]
  E --> F["Baseline may shrink"]
  F --> D
  B --> G["Expansion allowed only with rule introduction gate"]
  D --> H["CI and Habitat report normalized diagnostics"]
diagram.caption: Baselines are explicit state, not comments or allowlists hidden inside scripts.

blocks.explanation: This is what lets the repo adopt enforcement without pretending all historical debt is gone. Locked empty baselines fail immediately; non-empty baselines are shrink-only.

slides.explanation:
## Project-Plane Taxonomy

Nx tags make the workspace graph queryable and enforceable. The tags were derived from current rules and docs, then locked as project-plane law.

table.caption: Current `kind:*` vocabulary.
rows: Tag,Meaning,Owner plane
kind:app,User-facing apps and entry surfaces,Nx project graph
kind:sdk,High-level authoring and builder APIs,Nx project graph plus Grit subpath rules
kind:engine,Pure TS engine/domain logic,Nx graph plus runtime-value tests
kind:adapter,Sole owner of Civ7 globals and base-standard imports,Nx graph plus Grit/runtime import guards
kind:control,Live Civ7 control/session/service surfaces,Nx graph plus contract ownership rules
kind:foundation,Pure leaf libraries,Nx graph
kind:plugin,Reusable CLI/SDK helper libraries,Nx graph
kind:mod,Game-facing mod packages,Nx graph plus intra-mod Grit/file rules
kind:tooling,Repo-local development tooling,Nx graph

blocks.explanation: Nx owns dependencies between projects. Grit and file-layer rules own structure inside a project. The harness intentionally keeps those planes separate.

slides.explanation:
## Grit Is Native, Habitat Is the Adapter

H5 settled the Grit integration boundary after a lot of false starts: use native Grit patterns and tests, but keep Habitat responsible for rule IDs, baselines, and fail/pass semantics.

diagram.content:
flowchart LR
  A[".grit/patterns/habitat/**"] --> B["grit patterns test"]
  A --> C["one grit --json check over audit roots"]
  C --> D["Habitat maps findings to rule IDs"]
  D --> E["Baselines and locked-rule failure"]
  E --> F["Nx target and CI reporting"]
diagram.caption: Pattern correctness is native Grit. Enforcement semantics are Habitat.

table.caption: Grit lifecycle decisions.
rows: Decision,Reason
Patterns live under `.grit/patterns/habitat/**`,Uses Grit's native catalog location
`grit patterns test` is the sample authority,Avoids custom fixture runners
One native JSON scan per Habitat grit-check invocation,Avoids per-rule process startup and fake wrappers
Habitat parses JSON and decides failure,Raw `grit --json check` can exit 0 with findings
No temp workspaces or copied source,Those were overengineered and abandoned

slides.explanation:
## The Command Surface

The CLI is oclif. The tools underneath stay native. Habitat is the contract boundary an agent can remember.

table.caption: What can be done end to end now.
rows: Need,Command,What it gives you
Orient before editing,`bun run habitat classify <path-or-diff>`,Owning project, tags, rules, and required targets
Run structural checks,`bun run habitat:check`,All rule-pack diagnostics with baselines applied
Apply safe hygiene fixes,`bun run habitat:fix`,Biome format/import organization/safe assists
Run the integrated proof path,`bun run habitat:verify`,Habitat check plus Nx affected targets
Inspect project graph,`bun run habitat -- graph --json`,Machine-readable Nx graph
Run local staged hook,`bun run habitat hook pre-commit`,Staged file-layer, Biome, and Grit feedback
Run local affected push gate,`bun run habitat hook pre-push`,Committed-range affected feedback

slides.explanation:
## Agent Operating Loop

H8 turns the harness into a workflow, not only a gate.

diagram.content:
flowchart LR
  A["Receive task"] --> B["habitat classify path or diff"]
  B --> C{"Supported shape?"}
  C -->|Yes| D["Generate with Nx generator"]
  C -->|No| E["Use domain owner docs and code"]
  D --> F["Author change"]
  E --> F
  F --> G["habitat fix"]
  G --> H["Run classify-reported targets"]
  H --> I["Commit with hooks"]
  I --> J["Pre-push affected verify"]
diagram.caption: The default is not to guess. The default is to ask Habitat what structure you are touching.

objectives.title: Classify before authoring
objectives.description: Paths and diffs become explicit ownership and target information before code changes start.

keyResults.text: Use `bun run habitat classify <path-or-diff>` as the entry point.
Run at least the required targets Habitat reports.

objectives.title: Generate supported structure
objectives.description: Known uniform structures are created mechanically so tags, scripts, and tests start clean.

keyResults.text: Use the project generator for foundation, plugin, and app packages.
Use the pattern generator for new native Grit rules.

slides.explanation:
## What Generation Covers

H8 deliberately supports only uniform shapes. That refusal is part of the design: Habitat should not guess domain architecture.

table.caption: Generator coverage after H8.
rows: Generator,Supported now,Refused or deferred
`@internal/habitat-harness:project`,foundation / plugin / app,mod / engine / control / adapter / sdk / tooling
`@internal/habitat-harness:pattern`,Native Grit pattern + empty baseline + rule-pack entry,Semantic product rules or custom scanners
Harness migrations,No-op migration wiring via local run file,Published package migration flow
`habitat classify`,Paths and literal diff or patch inputs,Policy decisions outside known ownership data

blocks.explanation: The refusal path is valuable. Unsupported kinds are domain-owned shapes; Habitat should not create fake structure just because a generator can write files.

slides.explanation:
## Hooks Are Feedback, Not Truth

H7 adds local friction reduction without moving authority away from CI.

diagram.content:
flowchart TD
  A["git commit"] --> B["Husky pre-commit"]
  B --> C["Legacy resources publish behavior"]
  C --> D["Staged file-layer guards"]
  D --> E["Biome staged formatting/check"]
  E --> F["One path-scoped Grit check"]
  F --> G["Commit allowed"]
  H["git push"] --> I["Husky pre-push"]
  I --> J["Graphite parent or main merge-base"]
  J --> K["Nx affected hook target set"]
  K --> L["Push allowed"]
diagram.caption: Hooks use the same Habitat command surface, but CI remains authoritative.

blocks.explanation: Safety mattered: pre-commit refuses partially staged format-eligible files instead of formatting unstaged hunks, restages only formatter-touched files, and leaves `--no-verify` as a local escape hatch.

slides.explanation:
## What Was Proved

The workstream was closed by evidence classes, not optimistic summaries.

items.title: Native Grit catalog
items.description: Native `grit patterns test --verbose` passed after H6; H5 also proved subsecond pattern-test behavior.

items.title: Generated probes
items.description: Foundation, plugin, and app generator outputs passed cold Nx build, check, and test before removal.

items.title: Rule-pack closure
items.description: Final `habitat:check` passed with zero failing rules and one advisory doc-ambiguity finding.

items.title: Root closure
items.description: H6 closed the transition from duplicate enforcement to Habitat-owned structural enforcement.

slides.explanation:
## What Is Actually Finished

Locally, the train is closed. The repo now has a complete structural loop from orientation to enforcement.

table.caption: H1-H8 outcome map.
rows: Slice,Outcome
H1 Nx adoption,Nx replaced Turbo as graph/cache/task authority while preserving root workflows
H2 scaffold,Habitat package, rule pack, baselines, and CLI skeleton wrapped existing checks
H3 boundary tags,All projects tagged and project-plane constraints locked empty
H4 Biome hygiene,Biome became formatter/hygiene owner with Prettier retired
H4.5 oclif CLI,Habitat command surface moved to repo-standard oclif
H5 Grit catalog,Grit/file-layer rules added with native patterns and generated-zone guards
H6 consolidation,Duplicated scripts/ESLint/tests retired or slimmed only after parity proof
H7 hooks,Husky delegated staged and pre-push checks to Habitat
H8 generators/migrations/classify,Supported structures became generative and classify-first became the agent loop

slides.explanation:
## Caveats and Tradeoffs

The system is usable, but not magically complete. The important thing is that the remaining edges are named.

table.caption: Known caveats after local closure.
rows: Area,Caveat,What to do next
Graphite lifecycle,H1-H8 are locally closed; stack submission/drain remains,Submit or drain bottom-up through the repo's Graphite process
Lower stack note,`agent-F-habitat-harness-workstream` still shows needs restack,Inspect before stack submission instead of blindly rewriting
Broad hook timing,Broad root/Habitat self-edits can exceed narrow pre-push budget,Treat as an operational boundary; normal path is scoped classify/affected checks
Domain generators,mod/engine/control/adapter/sdk/tooling generators are refused,Let domain owners define real shapes before generating them
Habitat-native budget,Doc/manifest/cross-file checks remain native by design,Watch the degeneration trigger if this list grows
Strict domain-refactor profile,Full profile remains manual because it has existing findings,Promote families only with parity and baseline ceremony
Slides repo state,The slideshow schema repo is dirty with unrelated work,Use it for validation here; avoid mixing this artifact into that worktree without a separate branch

slides.explanation:
## What Comes Next

The harness makes structural work cheaper, but the next value comes from using it consistently.

objectives.title: Drain the stack cleanly
objectives.description: Local closure is not the same as merged authority.

keyResults.text: Submit Graphite branches with H1-H8 evidence preserved.
Resolve any stale lower-branch restack state before review.

objectives.title: Move structural checks to Habitat
objectives.description: The intended end state is not structural tests hiding in ordinary test suites.

keyResults.text: Retire remaining duplicate structural tests only after parity proof.
Use Grit, Biome, Nx, or file-layer ownership before adding native rules.

objectives.title: Grow generators deliberately
objectives.description: Future generators should encode real owner-approved shapes, not guesses.

keyResults.text: Define domain-owned generators for non-uniform project kinds.
Use migrations for convention changes once shapes are stable.

slides.explanation:
## Bottom Line

What we can do now: start from a path or diff, know the owning structure, generate supported scaffolds, enforce the architecture through native tool layers, keep baselines honest, get fast local feedback, and produce CI-readable proof.

explainer.content: The harness is a practical boundary object for agent work. It is strict enough to prevent drift, explicit enough to survive handoff, and native enough that each tool keeps doing the job it is good at. The value is not any single command; it is the whole loop becoming teachable and repeatable.

items.label: Create
items.subtitle: supported project and Grit rule shapes
